Self-hosted agent runtimes like OpenClaw are showing up fast in enterprise pilots, and they introduce a blunt reality: OpenClaw includes limited built-in security controls. The runtime can ingest untrusted text, download and execute skills (i.e. code) from external sources, and perform actions using the credentials assigned to it.

**Indirect prompt injection:** Attackers can hide malicious instructions inside content an agent reads and can either steer tool use or modify its memory to affect its behavior over time unless users put strong boundaries in place.
Use dedicated identities for agents. Minimize permissions. Prefer short-lived tokens. Use controlled consent for powerful permissions.

Because of these characteristics, OpenClaw should be treated as untrusted code execution with persistent credentials. It is not appropriate to run on a standard personal or enterprise workstation. If an organization determines that OpenClaw must be evaluated, it should be deployed only in a fully isolated environment such as a dedicated virtual machine or separate physical system. The runtime should use dedicated, non-privileged credentials and access only non-sensitive data.

excerpts: Network egress controls: Blocking network access to arbitrary sites prevents exfiltration of data or establishing a remote shell without additional exploits.

Block file writes outside of the workspace: Blocking write operations to files outside of the workspace prevents a number of persistence mechanisms, sandbox escapes, and remote code execution (RCE) techniques.

Block writes to configuration files, no matter where they are located: Blocking writes to config files prevents exploitation of hooks, skills, and local model context protocol (MCP) configurations that often run outside of a sandbox context.
Sandbox the entire integrated development environment (IDE) and all spawned functions (e.g., hooks, MCP startup scripts, skills, and tool calls), and, where possible, are run as their own user.
Use virtualization to isolate the sandbox kernel from the host kernel (e.g., microVM, Kata container, full VM)

Intermediate mitigations like gVisor, which mediate system calls via a separate user-space kernel, are preferable to fully shared solutions, but offer different and potentially weaker security guarantees than full virtualization.
Use a secret injection approach to prevent secrets (e.g., in environment variables) from being shared with the agent.

excerpts: **Prompt injections** are a common and dangerous type of attack. A prompt injection happens when untrusted text or data enters an AI system, and malicious contents in that text or data attempt to override instructions to the AI. The end goals of prompt injections vary but can include exfiltrating private data via downstream tool calls, taking misaligned actions, or otherwise changing model behavior in an unintended way.

**Private data leakage** , when an agent accidentally shares private data, is also a risk to guard against. It’s possible for a model to leak private data in a way that’s not intended, without an attacker behind it.
By defining structured outputs between nodes (e.g., enums, fixed schemas, required field names), you eliminate freeform channels that attackers can exploit to smuggle instructions or data.
Sanitize incoming inputs using built-in guardrails to redact personally identifiable information (PII) and detect jailbreak attempts.
When using MCP tools, always enable tool approvals so end users can review and confirm every operation, including reads and writes. In Agent Builder, use the human approval node.

Use the following guidance to reduce the attack surface and mitigate these risks. However, *even with these mitigations* , agents won’t be perfect and can still make mistakes or be tricked; as a result, it’s important to understand these risks and use caution in what access you give agents and how you apply agents.

OpenHands Agent SDK: Provides native support for distributed deployments where agents run on client machines while tools execute in remote sandboxed environments. This architecture enables:
•Separation of concerns: Agent logic runs locally (low latency, private), while tool execution runs remotely (isolated, scalable)
•Resource flexibility: High-compute tools (e.g., code compilation, browser automation) run on remote servers without client hardware constraints
•Security isolation: Untrusted code executes in remote containers, protecting client machines
•Multi-tenancy: Multiple users share remote infrastructure while maintaining workspace isolation

OpenHands: First-class Docker runtime support that isolates code execution in containers, providing:
•Complete filesystem isolation
•Network policy enforcement
•Resource limits (CPU, memory, disk)
•Easy reproducibility across environments
G

I agents can perform risky actions, making security especially important when they run on a user’s machine. To address this, SDK treats security as a first-class concern within the agent’s control loop. Two abstractions form the core of this design: the SecurityAnalyzer , which rates each tool call as low, medium, high, or unknown risk, and the ConfirmationPolicy , which determines whether user approval is required before execution based on the action’s details and assessed risk

The SDK includes a built-in pair: LLMSecurityAnalyzer , which appends a security_risk field to tool calls, and ConfirmRisky policy, which blocks actions exceeding a configurable risk threshold (default: high)
enHands Agent SDK: Implements multi-layer security where the LLM analyzes each action before execution, assigning risk levels ( LOW , MEDIUM , HIGH ) based on system impac

•Confirmation policies: Based on risk level, the system triggers appropriate workflows, such as:
–LOW: Execute immediately
–HIGH: Pause execution, request human confirmation
Customizable policies: Developers define ConfirmationPolicy classes to match their risk tolerance (e.g., auto-approve in sandbox environment, require confirmation in production).

E2B uses Firecracker microVMs to provide hardware-level isolation for each code execution.
Each code execution runs in its own microVM, which means it gets a dedicated Linux kernel.
This kernel-level isolation prevents exploits that rely on shared kernel vulnerabilities, such as container escape attacks that target the host operating system.

**Docker containers** (used by Daytona) isolate processes using Linux kernel features like namespaces and cgroups. Multiple containers share the same kernel, which means kernel vulnerabilities could potentially allow an attacker to escape container boundaries.

OpenHands Agent SDK: Provides native support for distributed deployments where agents run on client machines while tools execute in remote sandboxed environments. This architecture enables:
•Separation of concerns: Agent logic runs locally (low latency, private), while tool execution runs remotely (isolated, scalable)
•Resource flexibility: High-compute tools (e.g., code compilation, browser automation) run on remote servers without client hardware constraints
•Security isolation: Untrusted code executes in remote containers, protecting client machines
•Multi-tenancy: Multiple users share remote infrastructure while maintaining workspace isolation

I agents can perform risky actions, making security especially important when they run on a user’s machine. To address this, SDK treats security as a first-class concern within the agent’s control loop. Two abstractions form the core of this design: the SecurityAnalyzer , which rates each tool call as low, medium, high, or unknown risk, and the ConfirmationPolicy , which determines whether user approval is required before execution based on the action’s details and assessed risk

The SDK includes a built-in pair: LLMSecurityAnalyzer , which appends a security_risk field to tool calls, and ConfirmRisky policy, which blocks actions exceeding a configurable risk threshold (default: high)
enHands Agent SDK: Implements multi-layer security where the LLM analyzes each action before execution, assigning risk levels ( LOW , MEDIUM , HIGH ) based on system impac

•Confirmation policies: Based on risk level, the system triggers appropriate workflows, such as:
–LOW: Execute immediately
–HIGH: Pause execution, request human confirmation

excerpts: The big Fly Machine trick is: starting up super fast; like, "in response to an HTTP request from a user" fast.
Scale a workload out horizontally with `fly machine clone` :

You can clone a specific Machine as many times as you like, into specific regions; you can manage each of those Machines
individually with `fly machine stop` and `fly machine start`

nce the Fly Machine exists, we boot it up for you right away. The Machine is in `started` state. It’s running. You can talk to it. This happens fast! Everything’s already assembled; we’re just booting. Usually this takes *well under a second* .

excerpts:
t:
OpenHands Agent SDK: Provides native support for distributed deployments where agents run on client machines while tools execute in remote sandboxed environments. This architecture enables:
•Separation of concerns: Agent logic runs locally (low latency, private), while tool execution runs remotely (isolated, scalable)
•Resource flexibility: High-compute tools (e.g., code compilation, browser automation) run on remote servers without client hardware constraints
•Security isolation: Untrusted code executes in remote containers, protecting client machines
•Multi-tenancy: Multiple users share remote infrastructure while maintaining workspace isolation

:
OpenHands: First-class Docker runtime support that isolates code execution in containers, providing:
•Complete filesystem isolation
•Network policy enforcement
•Resource limits (CPU, memory, disk)
•Easy reproducibility across environments
G

I agents can perform risky actions, making security especially important when they run on a user’s machine. To address this, SDK treats security as a first-class concern within the agent’s control loop. Two abstractions form the core of this design: the SecurityAnalyzer , which rates each tool call as low, medium, high, or unknown risk, and the ConfirmationPolicy , which determines whether user approval is required before execution based on the action’s details and assessed risk
OpenHands Agent SDK: Provides native support for distributed deployments where agents run on client machines while tools execute in remote sandboxed environments.
Customizable policies: Developers define ConfirmationPolicy classes to match their risk tolerance (e.g., auto-approve in sandbox environment, require confirmation in production).

The SDK includes a built-in pair: LLMSecurityAnalyzer , which appends a security_risk field to tool calls, and ConfirmRisky policy, which blocks actions exceeding a configurable risk threshold (default: high)
enHands Agent SDK: Implements multi-layer security where the LLM analyzes each action before execution, assigning risk levels ( LOW , MEDIUM , HIGH ) based on system impac

•Confirmation policies: Based on risk level, the system triggers appropriate workflows, such as:
–LOW: Execute immediately
–HIGH: Pause execution, request human confirmation

basis.reasoning: The target use case describes a persistent, development-oriented sandbox where agents can edit files and execute commands in a remote, isolated environment. Excerpts that lay out an OpenHands-style architecture explicitly separate agent logic from tool execution by running tools in remote sandboxed environments, with Docker-based isolation, filesystem and network separation, and configurable risk-based policies. These points directly map to a persistent remote runtime for agent frameworks, since they describe the security-first, isolation-focused design that enables ongoing development work in a cloud sandbox. The Daytona reference shows a real-world platform positioning itself as a secure, isolated cloud environment intended as a remote runtime for agent frameworks like OpenHands, which closely matches the desired primary use case. Additional OpenHands excerpts elaborate on concrete mechanisms: a native Docker runtime that isolates code execution with complete filesystem isolation, network policy enforcement, and resource limits; a security-centric control loop with risk analysis and a ConfirmationPolicy that governs whether actions run automatically or require human approval. Together, these excerpts establish a coherent architecture for a persistent, secure development workspace for AI agents, including how tool execution is isolated, how credentials and risk are managed, and how reproducibility and multi-tenant isolation are achieved. The E2B and Claude Code sandbox references provide complementary evidence of similar sandboxing approaches (on-demand Linux VMs, gVisor-like isolation, and secure sandboxing strategies) that reinforce the broader feasibility and design patterns for persistent agent runtimes, though they are somewhat less central to the explicit OpenHands-based persistent-workspace use case. Overall, the strongest support comes from explicit OpenHands descriptions of remote sandboxed execution and security controls, reinforced by Daytona’s stated role as a secure remote runtime, with additional corroboration from related sandboxing platforms and policy mechanisms.

The second-most relevant excerpt discusses that Modal provides gVisor containers with persistent storage and notes the SDK-defined images constraint, which corroborates the secure sandboxing model and runtime execution environment for code, albeit with a caveat about image requirements. The least relevant excerpt also mentions Modal in the context of a table, confirming Modal as a gVisor-based sandbox option and its relation to ML/AI workloads, but it provides less direct detail about runtime dynamic container creation and untrusted code execution compared to the other two excerpts. Together, these excerpts support the field value’s core claim that Modal enables secure, runtime containerized execution for ML/AI workloads and potentially for running arbitrary code, including code generated by an LLM or tests against repositories, within a sandboxed environment.

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the state of agentic AI tool execution in 2025-2026, summarizing the shift towards stronger isolation, the primary security threats, and the architectural patterns being adopted by production systems.

tool_execution_architecture_patterns.description: Describes the latest architectural patterns for tool execution in LLM agents, including dependency injection, remote execution in sandboxed environments, and the design of agent control loops that incorporate security checks.

threat_type.description: The category of the security threat (e.g., Indirect Prompt Injection, Malicious Tool Supply Chain).

description.description: An explanation of how the threat manifests, such as attackers hiding instructions in content an agent reads.

example_vector.description: A concrete example of an attack vector, such as a poisoned document, a malicious repository, or a compromised skill from a public registry.

technology_category.description: The class of isolation technology, such as MicroVMs, Application Kernels, Containers, or Language-level Isolates.

example_implementation.description: A specific example of the technology, such as Firecracker, gVisor, Docker, or WASM/V8.

isolation_boundary.description: The security boundary provided by the technology (e.g., Hardware Virtualization, Kernel API, Process Namespaces).

security_strength.description: The relative security strength, noting if it prevents kernel-level exploits or only provides process-level isolation.

micro_vm_sandboxing.description: A detailed look at microVM technology, particularly Firecracker, explaining how it provides strong hardware-enforced isolation with a dedicated kernel per sandbox, and its adoption by platforms like E2B, Fly.io, and AWS Lambda.

application_kernel_sandboxing.description: An explanation of userspace kernel technology like gVisor, which intercepts and mediates system calls to provide a security boundary without full hardware virtualization. Used by platforms like Modal and Claude Code.

container_and_isolate_sandboxing.description: Discusses the use of traditional containers (e.g., Docker in OpenHands) and lightweight isolates (WASM, Deno, Cloudflare Workers/V8) for tool execution, highlighting their performance benefits and security trade-offs compared to heavier isolation.

credential_management_and_injection.description: Covers how production systems securely handle credentials, including techniques like injecting secrets as environment variables, automatic masking in logs and LLM context, and the use of short-lived, narrowly-scoped tokens.

result_sanitization_and_policy_enforceme.description: Details methods for securing the data flow to and from tools, including sanitizing inputs to prevent prompt injection, validating structured outputs against a schema, and enforcing network egress policies (allow-lists).

capability_based_security_and_approvals.description: Explores approaches to managing agent permissions, including capability-based models like the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and the critical role of human-in-the-loop confirmation for high-risk actions.

concurrency_and_parallelism_strategies.description: Addresses patterns for managing concurrent and parallel tool execution in agentic workflows, including the use of workflow orchestrators and the challenges of maintaining security and state consistency across multiple simultaneous tool calls.

isolation_technology.description: The primary sandboxing technology used, specifically its gVisor-style runtime for task isolation.

credential_handling.description: How the system manages credentials, such as using an Anthropic-managed proxy for Git operations to keep tokens off the sandbox.

policy_enforcement.description: The mechanism for enforcing security policies, including strict allow-lists for network/filesystem access and auto-approval for trusted resources.

primary_sandbox.description: The name and nature of the primary sandboxing environment provided, such as the Code Interpreter sandbox.

data_flow_control_method.description: The recommended technique for constraining data flow and preventing prompt injection, such as using structured outputs (e.g., enums, fixed schemas).

user_oversight_mechanism.description: The method for ensuring user consent for actions, specifically the use of a 'human approval node' in workflows.

risk_assessment_component.description: The component responsible for analyzing action risk, the 'SecurityAnalyzer', which uses an LLM to assign risk levels (low, medium, high).

approval_policy_component.description: The component that enforces user confirmation based on risk, the 'ConfirmationPolicy', which can be customized.

sandboxing_technology.description: The underlying sandboxing technology used, which is first-class support for Docker containers to provide filesystem and network isolation.

secrets_management_feature.description: The built-in system for handling secrets, which includes automatic detection, injection as environment variables, and masking in logs.

devin_and_proprietary_agents_analysis.description: An assessment of highly autonomous agents like Devin, NanoClaw, and IronClaw. This section notes the general lack of public information regarding their specific internal security, isolation, and sandboxing mechanisms.

official_risk_posture.description: Microsoft's official assessment of the risk, which is to treat the runtime as 'untrusted code execution with persistent credentials'.

recommended_environment.description: The type of environment recommended for running OpenClaw, such as a fully isolated dedicated VM or a separate physical system.

key_mitigations.description: A summary of the essential security guardrails, including using dedicated non-privileged credentials, monitoring for state manipulation, and having a rapid rebuild plan.

platform_name.description: The name of the sandbox-as-a-service provider (e.g., E2B, Modal, Fly.io, Daytona).

isolation_technology.description: The core isolation technology the platform is built on (e.g., Firecracker microVMs, gVisor, Docker containers).

persistence_model.description: How the platform handles state between sessions (e.g., Ephemeral with a time limit, Stateful/Long-living, Configurable).

primary_use_case.description: The target use case the platform is optimized for (e.g., AI agent tools, ML/AI workloads, persistent development workspaces).

sandbox_as_a_service_platforms.description: Details on platforms that provide secure, cloud-based execution environments specifically for AI agents. Each item will profile a specific service, including E2B (Firecracker), Modal (gVisor), and Fly.io (Firecracker), covering their isolation tech, persistence models, and ideal use cases.

unaddressed_challenges_in_tool_execution.description: Directly addresses what might be missing from current approaches, discussing unsolved problems like secure inter-agent communication, assessing cumulative risk from chained tool calls, and effective lifecycle management for sandbox environments to prevent state pollution.

standard_or_initiative.description: The name of the standard or guidance, such as the OWASP Agentic Security Initiative (ASI) or NIST's Agent Hijacking Evaluations.

issuing_organization.description: The organization responsible for the standard, such as OWASP or NIST.

focus_area.description: The primary goal of the standard, such as defining a threat taxonomy (ASI01-ASI10) or developing adaptive evaluation metrics.

identifier.description: The CVE ID (e.g., CVE-2025-3248) or a descriptive name for the incident.

affected_system.description: The software, framework, or platform that was vulnerable (e.g., Langflow, Open WebUI).

vulnerability_summary.description: A brief description of the vulnerability, such as 'unauthenticated remote code execution' or 'malicious model server to token theft'.

architectural_lesson.description: The underlying architectural weakness the incident highlights, such as chained failures, weak input validation, or misplaced trust boundaries.
